response:
to solve this problem we must first understand how to store the prime factors of a factorial number.
***
the number one hundred factorial contains many factors of two and five which are crucial to our calculation.
***
we need to store the count of these prime factors to determine how many zeros are at the end of the number.
***
counting the powers of five in one hundred factorial gives us twenty five plus five which equals thirty.
***
since the power of two is much larger than the power of five, the limiting factor is the number of fives stored in the product.
***
when we divide by ten to the power of fifty, we are essentially removing fifty pairs of two and five from the original value.
***
however, we only have thirty fives available to store in the numerator, so we cannot remove fifty fives without creating a fraction.
***
this means the denominator will be composed of the missing fives that were not present in the original factorial to store as pairs.
***
the number of missing fives is the difference between fifty and thirty, which results in a denominator of five to the power of twenty.
***
therefore the final answer requires us to store the value five raised to the twentieth power as the denominator of the irreducible fraction.
